# Upstream Reconciliation and Open-Source Customization Maintenance Protocol

This protocol manages long-term adoption relationships between AIOS or a project and external software, including ordinary dependencies, adapters, overlays, patch queues, and maintained forks.

Core principles:

```text
Forks are a normal way to collaborate and host work;
Prioritize reducing unnecessary long-term source differences, not avoiding GitHub Forks;
Use upstream extension points, adapters, or overlays when possible instead of expanding the core patch surface;
When long-term differences form, record an immutable upstream base and continuously look for paths to reduce, upstream, or retire them;
Git mergeability does not imply behavioral equivalence;
Recommendation, authorization, implementation, validation, and deployment must be separated;
Any automated update must have validation and a recoverable boundary.
```

## 1. Scope and Non-Goals

Applies to:

- external CLIs, libraries, services, Web UIs, MCP servers, skills, or vendored components;
- local adapters, sidecars, overlays, and patch queues;
- long-term forks that need to continuously track upstream;
- local differences that may be replaced by equivalent upstream capabilities.

Not mandatory for:

- purely first-party project bodies without an external upstream;
- one-off temporary changes;
- ordinary dependencies with no local differences and sufficiently managed by an existing lockfile/manifest.

This protocol is not a new fork maintainer platform, central component database, or deployment system. The first version reuses project repositories, Git, existing CI, LLL, AIOS registry, and OPS vault.

## 2. Domain Model

### 2.1 Two Dimensions of Forks

“Fork” can mean a repository hosting relationship on GitHub/GitLab, or it can mean long-term maintenance of local source differences. These two must be separated:

- **repository topology**: where the code is hosted and how it relates to upstream in Git; a GitHub Fork is a normal and common workspace for customization.
- **divergence strategy**: whether and how local behavior or source differences are maintained; long-term divergence, not clicking Fork itself, is what creates ongoing reconciliation cost.

Therefore, `github-fork + adapter`, `github-fork + no-source-delta`, and `independent-clone + maintained-source-divergence` are all valid combinations. The protocol does not aim to avoid Forks; it aims to control, explain, and continuously reduce unnecessary local differences.

### 2.2 Orthogonal Classification

Do not use a single `class` to mix source, adoption style, and importance.

| Dimension | Example values | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| provenance | `first-party` / `external-upstream` | Who owns the original component |
| repository topology | `upstream-direct` / `github-fork` / `independent-clone` / `mirror` | Repository hosting, contribution, and fetch relationship |
| divergence strategy | `no-source-delta` / `adapter` / `overlay` / `patch-queue` / `maintained-source-divergence` | How local differences are implemented and maintained |
| criticality | `utility` / `infra` / `core-control-plane` | Validation, authorization, and update cadence |
| runtime ownership | `app-owned` / `aios-managed` / `user-owned` | Who can install, deploy, restart, and roll back |
| visibility | `public` / `private` / `mixed-by-reference` | Whether facts belong in a public repo or with a private owner |

### 2.3 Core Objects

| Object | Responsibilities | Should not carry |
|---|---|---|
| Component | Upstream software or module with stable identity | A one-time sync state machine |
| Adoption | How a local project/instance adopts a Component; aggregate root | Secrets or complete production receipts |
| Base Revision | Current accepted immutable upstream version | Floating branch/channel |
| Invariant | Behavioral, security, or boundary condition that must remain true after reconciliation | Unverified wishes or ordinary preferences |
| Local Delta | Intentional local product/behavior difference | The state of a specific candidate or decision |
| Upstream Candidate | Snapshot of an upstream change that may affect an Adoption | Confirmed equivalence or authorized adoption |
| Reconciliation Case | One evaluation of a Candidate, Delta, and Invariant | Product authorization or deployment receipt |
| Decision Record | Disposition authorization by a Human or explicit policy | Unauthorized machine execution |
| Change Set | Exact changes and undo plan prepared after a decision | Treating a proposal as an applied fact |
| Validation Evidence | Check evidence for an exact revision/change set | Hiding scope behind “CI green” |
| Deployment Projection | Project/OPS release, health, and rollback for validated artifacts | Internal state machine of the general reconciliation aggregate |

```text
Component
  └─ Adoption (owner + repository topology + divergence strategy + accepted base)
       ├─ Invariant*
       ├─ Local Delta*
       └─ Reconciliation Case*
            ├─ Upstream Candidate
            ├─ Git result + behavioral relationship
            ├─ Decision Record
            ├─ Change Set
            └─ Validation Evidence

Deployment Projection --references--> validated revision/change set
```

### 2.4 Four Independent Judgments

| Judgment | Typical results | Does not imply |
|---|---|---|
| Git applicability | `clean` / `text-conflict` / `not-applicable` / `unknown` | `clean` does not mean behaviorally compatible; conflict does not mean product conflict |
| behavioral relationship | `unrelated` / `complementary` / `partial-overlap` / `equivalent` / `conflicting` / `unknown` | `equivalent` does not mean upstream must be adopted |
| product disposition | `accept-upstream` / `keep-local` / `hybrid` / `defer` / `retire-local` / `reject-update` | A decision does not mean implementation is correct or deployed |
| deployment | `not-requested` / `pending-approval` / `deploying` / `healthy` / `failed` / `rolled-back` | Merge/validation does not mean production authorization |

`equivalent` only means that, within an explicitly declared scope of API, CLI, configuration, data, permissions, security, performance, or failure modes, referenced evidence verifies that the same observable contract is satisfied. Anything outside the scope remains unknown.

### 2.5 Minimal Lifecycle

- Adoption: `tracked | paused | retired`
- The current Adoption contract only lists active Local Deltas that still need maintenance. After a Delta is retired by a Human/Policy Decision and validated, remove it from the active list. Keep minimal historical evidence in the Decision Record, Git history, or L2 `decisions/`; stable IDs must not be reused.
- Reconciliation Case:

```text
detected → assessed → no-action | decision-required
decision-required → authorized | deferred | closed
deferred → assessed | closed
authorized → implemented → validated → closed
```

A Decision Record’s `status` and product disposition are two dimensions:

- `approved` means the selected disposition has been authorized; outcomes that require changes, such as `accept-upstream`, `hybrid`, and `retire-local`, enter `authorized`, while outcomes that require no changes, such as `keep-local` and `reject-update`, are recorded with evidence and then go directly to `closed`;
- `deferred` corresponds to `defer`; the Case enters `deferred` with `revisit_after` or `revisit_trigger`, and returns to `assessed` when triggered;
- `rejected` only rejects the current Decision proposal. It is not automatically the same as `reject-update`. By default, the Case remains `decision-required` until a new Decision supersedes it or it is explicitly closed;
- `superseded` only means this Decision Record was replaced by a more precise new record; it is not the business terminal state of the Case.

Delta retirement must reference a Decision; do not permanently pile retired tombstones into the current active contract for historical completeness.

## 3. Invariants

1. **Unambiguous baseline**: the accepted base uses a commit, digest, or equivalent immutable identity; branches, channels, and tags may only be tracking refs.
2. **Git and behavior are orthogonal**: a clean merge is not automatically recorded as compatible, equivalent, or safe.
3. **Intent and implementation are separate**: a Delta’s intent/behavior contract is not lost because a patch becomes an adapter, files are refactored, or commits are squashed.
4. **Equivalence has scope and evidence**: relevant Invariants, validation, comparison scope, and unknowns must be referenced; insufficient evidence is `unknown`.
5. **Recommendation is not authorization**: Agent recommendations, confidence, labels, Issue states, and CI results cannot replace Human/Policy authority.
6. **Application and deployment are separate**: apply/merge/validate does not automatically authorize production release or service restart.
7. **Single source of truth, rebuildable projections**: GitHub, CI, README, boards, and Viewers only reference stable IDs.
8. **Private facts do not enter the public protocol**: hosts, secrets, environments, deployed revisions, and receipts belong to the project’s private owner or live OPS.
9. **Originals and evidence are retained**: when extracting assets from a Worksite, derive/copy by default and do not overwrite the source.
10. **Fail closed**: when base, owner, Invariant, validation, or authorization is missing, only observe, draft, or report `unknown/blocked`.

## 4. Source of Truth and Owners

| Location | Should own | Should not own |
|---|---|---|
| Project repo | Adoption contract, accepted base, Delta, Invariant, validation entry points, long-term Decision/Change refs | Secrets or cross-instance current deployment state |
| `aios-kit` | Public terminology, protocol, templates, review rules, optional validator | User-private runtime facts or all external source code |
| AIOS registry | Discoverable identity, typed locator, role/status, canonical record locator | Complete patch inventory or a second state machine |
| live OPS vault | service/environment, authorization boundary, secret refs, deployed revision, runbook, health/rollback receipts | Duplicate copy of the public protocol |
| LLL Worksite | One-time Candidate comparison, investigation, temporary evidence, review, handoff | Sole copy of the long-term project contract |
| GitHub/CI | PRs, Issues, Checks, reviews, triggers, and collaboration projections | Sole source of truth for Delta/Decision or deployment health |

Recommended write order:

```text
LLL proposal/evidence
  → Human/Policy Decision
  → project Adoption/Delta/Decision record
  → clean CI/build validation
  → separately authorized deployment
  → runtime readback
  → OPS current state/history
  → GitHub/Viewer refresh
```

## 5. Project Onboarding and File Contract

### 5.1 Agent onboarding

When creating an Adoption, the Agent first automatically discovers retrievable facts: upstream locator, default branch/ref, current base, repository topology, manifest/lockfile, test entry points, and deployment references. Do not hand these mechanical questions to a Human.

The Agent only asks about project-level decisions that truly affect product or authorization boundaries, and writes the answers into the project contract:

1. Update cadence: default `on-demand`; after the first successful reconciliation, whether to enable weekly/monthly observation. Use Adoption `paused` to pause tracking; do not create a separate `manual` cadence that overlaps with on-demand.
2. Merge authority: default `guarded-after-baseline`; the first reconciliation only proposes and must be approved by a Human. Strictly limited low-risk automatic merges are only allowed after the baseline is established.
3. Product boundaries: which Invariants must not be lost, which Local Deltas are intentional differences, and when product tradeoffs are needed as upstream approaches.
4. Deployment boundaries: whether merge still requires separate deployment, restart, migration, or production approval.

The default product preference is `upstream-first-with-quality-floor`: when upstream capabilities satisfy the declared behavior, quality, and security boundaries, prefer reducing or retiring local differences; keep local differences when evidence is insufficient or the local implementation is still clearly more appropriate.

### 5.2 L0: No Local Source Differences

- The actual version pin / accepted dependency fact is managed by the project manifest, lockfile, or its explicit owner.
- The AIOS registry only stores discovery entry points and the canonical record locator; it does not carry a second version source of truth.
- Repository topology can be a GitHub Fork, independent clone, or direct upstream use; whether it is a Fork does not determine whether source differences exist.
- Adding `UPSTREAM.md`, a patch queue, or a scheduled workflow is not mandatory.

### 5.3 L1: Adapter, Overlay, Patch, or Protected Invariant

Add a canonical `UPSTREAM.md` to the project repo, using YAML front matter for minimal machine fields and the body to explain human semantics. Do not create a separate YAML source of truth with overlapping content.

Template: [`templates/upstream-reconciliation/UPSTREAM.md`](../../../templates/upstream-reconciliation/UPSTREAM.md)

Minimum content:

- component/adoption identity;
- upstream locator, tracking ref, accepted immutable base;
- repository topology, divergence strategy, criticality, owner, cadence, and merge policy;
- Invariant and validation refs;
- Local Delta intent, behavior scope, realization refs, and retire condition;
- deployment impact policy/runbook reference;
- unknowns, not-checked items, and current Decision references.

Real secrets, hosts, production shells, and runtime state must not be written into the public contract.

### 5.4 L2: Multiple Deltas or Real Machine-Maintenance Pressure

Only split files when maintaining a single file creates real friction:

```text
UPSTREAM.md                 # entry point, human summary, machine record index
upstream/
  adoption.yaml             # machine canonical: adoption/base/index
  deltas/D001.yaml          # machine canonical: individual deltas
  decisions/<decision-id>.md
```

After upgrading to L2, `upstream/adoption.yaml` and `upstream/deltas/*.yaml` are the machine-canonical field owners; duplicate fields in `UPSTREAM.md` must be references or generated content, not a second manually maintained source of truth.

Upgrade triggers: repeatedly redoing the same patch, sustained growth in Deltas/conflicts, validation and retirement records becoming hard to maintain, or multiple automation consumers needing a stable schema. Do not build validators, databases, or daemons in advance for imagined needs.

## 6. Risk and Automation

### 6.1 Risks R0–R4

| Level | Typical changes | Default gates |
|---|---|---|
| R0 Informational | Non-normative documentation, observation metadata; no machine behavior change | lint/link; low-friction merge can be policy-driven |
| R1 Low risk | Test-covered internal refactor/build improvement; no interface, default, or permission changes | CI + diff; apply may be pre-authorized only for non-core projects |
| R2 Medium risk | User behavior, configuration defaults, minor dependencies, adapter/overlay, reversible format changes | Human review + specific Invariant/smoke; no auto-merge by default |
| R3 High risk | API, auth, workflow permissions, supply chain, state machine, release path, core UI/control plane, schema | Explicit Decision, security review, staging, production approval |
| R4 Severe/irreversible | Secrets/sovereignty boundaries, production infrastructure, data deletion/overwrite, irreversible migration, root/wide-area actuator | Automation only observes/prepares; each execution requires explicit authorization |

Raise the level for core components, insufficient tests, unpracticed rollback, weak provenance, or AI changes that cross policy/secret consumer/deployment paths. Few files, no conflicts, or a well-known upstream cannot lower the level.

### 6.2 Automation A0–A4

| Level | May do | Must not do |
|---|---|---|
| A0 Human-actuated | Human triggers, machine performs deterministic actions | schedule mutation |
| A1 Observe | Discover, fetch metadata, diff, classify, notify | Modify repo/service |
| A2 Propose | candidate branch/PR, Decision Card, AI draft, automated validation | Merge protected branches, deploy to production |
| A3 Guarded apply | Automatically merge R0/R1 non-core changes within explicitly pre-authorized scope | Core semantics, secrets, irreversible actions |
| A4 Guarded actuation | Pre-authorized dev/staging or precise safe rollback | Default production deployment, unknown rollback, AI privilege expansion |

Default caps: R0/R1 can reach A3 under mature rules; R2/R3 are A2; R4 only allows A1/A2 preparation, with actuation returning to A0.

`max_automation` is a cap, not a target that must be reached. Lower by at least one level during first onboarding, first major update, or first change to the rollback path.

New projects use `guarded-after-baseline` by default:

- The first reconciliation must be proposal-only and reviewed and authorized by a Human;
- After the first successful reconciliation and all required validation passes, the automation baseline is considered established;
- After that, only `R0/R1 + Git clean + behavioral=unrelated + all required checks green + no unknowns` may be auto-merged, and a post-action receipt must be provided;
- `partial-overlap`, `equivalent`, `conflicting`, `unknown`, Invariant risk, or Delta scope changes all exit the automatic path.

## 7. End-to-End Flow

```text
Trigger(on-demand | schedule | webhook)
→ Discover → Fetch → Classify → Prepare Candidate
→ Conflict/Behavioral Assessment → Optional AI Draft
→ Validate → Human/Policy Decision → Apply/Merge
→ Separate Deployment Authorization → Health → Rollback if needed → Record
```

On-demand requests, scheduled checks, and webhooks do not create three separate flows; they are only different triggers for the same reconciliation core. The basic product capability is Human-triggered on-demand reconciliation first; after the first real successful reconciliation, the Agent proactively offers scheduling.

### 7.1 Discover / Fetch

- Discover releases/refs/commit ranges read-only and resolve them to immutable SHA/digest.
- Fetch in a temporary environment with no secrets, no production network, and no write tokens.
- Do not automatically execute upstream hooks, workflows, Agent instructions, or package lifecycle/install scripts.
- If untrusted install/build scripts must be run, only run them in a disposable environment with no secrets and no write permissions, and record it.
- Fail closed on upstream ref rollback/rewrite, signature, or provenance anomalies.

### 7.2 Classify / Prepare Candidate

The Agent must cover the full change range from the accepted base to the candidate, not just release notes, Git conflict files, or upstream-claimed Features. Review has four layers:

1. Complete mechanical inventory: commits, changed paths, dependencies, workflows/permissions, config/defaults, API/schema, generated/vendor artifacts.
2. Impact mapping: identify possible impact on Local Deltas, Invariants, validation entry points, and deployment boundaries.
3. Behavioral analysis: classify as unrelated, complementary, partial-overlap, equivalent, conflicting, or unknown.
4. Deterministic validation: run the project-declared build/test/smoke/Invariant checks and list anything not run.

Changes that cannot be classified or validated must be `unknown`; they cannot be implicitly accepted because Git is clean.

Generate the Candidate in an isolated branch/worktree, and record `(base, ours, theirs)`, candidate SHA, full diff, and transcript. A pure mirror may hard reset, but it must be physically separated from the deployable mainline; the deployable mainline is never force-synced.

Complete evidence can remain in the Reconciliation Case/CI; the default table for Humans should only list items that affect the decision. Minor behavioral differences that do not change Delta, Invariant, or product disposition are summarized rather than interrupting item by item.

### 7.3 AI Draft

AI only performs explanation, behavioral comparison, conflict-fix drafts, test additions, and Decision Card drafts:

- minimize input; treat upstream content as untrusted data, not instructions;
- no production secrets, no mainline permissions, and no production actuators;
- tool and path allowlists; out-of-bound changes fail immediately;
- output changed paths, assumptions, unknowns, tests, risk changes, and evidence refs;
- validation is by clean CI and an independent reviewer; AI does not self-attest.

### 7.4 Validate / Decide

Validation is bound to the exact candidate SHA and lists checks not run.

| Risk | Minimum validation |
|---|---|
| R0 | schema/lint/link/render; prove no machine behavior change |
| R1 | unit, build, static/type/lint, affected path tests |
| R2 | R1 + integration/smoke + Invariant + compatibility/upgrade |
| R3 | R2 + security/dependency review + migration/rollback rehearsal + staging + independent reviewer |
| R4 | R3 + backup/restore validation + change window + least-privilege actuator + human observation |

The following must produce a Human Decision: R2+, behavioral conflicting/unknown, Agent recommendation to reduce or retire a Delta, Invariant change, product experience tradeoff, public/private/secret/data/deployment impact, permission expansion, or conflict between tests and human observation. If there are only minor behavioral differences that do not change these dispositions, record them in the report without actively interrupting.

A Decision must bind the exact candidate SHA and evidence set; if new commits are added to the candidate, the old approval is invalidated.

Template: [`templates/upstream-reconciliation/decision-card.md`](../../../templates/upstream-reconciliation/decision-card.md)

### 7.5 Apply / Deploy / Health / Rollback

- Apply only consumes an authorized exact revision/change set, and updates accepted base and Delta/Decision refs.
- Merge and deploy are two authorization surfaces.
- Deployment uses build-once/deploy-by-digest; production defaults to a Human/environment gate.
- Private actuators only consume schematized parameters and exact SHA/digest; they do not execute AI-generated shell.
- Health verifies at least one key Invariant, not just process liveness.
- previous-known-good, candidate, and current versions must be precisely identifiable.
- Rollback stops and returns to a Human if it touches irreversible data or unknown secrets.

## 8. GitHub Actions, Self-Hosted Agents, and Supply Chain

| Work | GitHub-hosted ephemeral | Self-hosted Agent/runner | Private actuator | Human |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Upstream discovery, diff, public PR CI | Default preferred option, no production secrets | Disable persistent runners for public/untrusted PRs | Not needed | Take over on exceptions |
| AI analysis/drafting | Optional, least privilege | Private code can use an isolated temporary environment | Must not call directly | Trigger/review |
| Private-network integration/staging | Produces approved artifact | Dedicated ephemeral/JIT runner | Restricted execution | Approve boundary |
| Production deployment | environment gate/orchestration | Does not run untrusted code | Consumes exact digest + schema parameters | Approval required by default |

Security baseline:

- workflows start from `permissions: {}` or `contents: read`, then elevate per job with least privilege;
- pin third-party Actions to full commit SHA; when updating, review the release, `action.yml`, runtime, and permissions;
- avoid privileged `pull_request_target` checkout;
- verify cross-workflow artifacts by source repo, workflow, event, head SHA, run conclusion, and digest;
- PR/Issue/upstream text must not be directly interpolated into shell or JavaScript;
- untrusted and privileged jobs must not share writable caches;
- persistent self-hosted runners must not execute public or unreviewed PRs; for private-network tests, prefer ephemeral/JIT and dedicated runner groups;
- for production, prefer OIDC/short-lived credentials; secret values must not enter AI prompts, artifacts, caches, PRs, command parameters, or health responses;
- packages, binaries, containers, submodules, LFS, generated code, and releases/tags must be checked according to project risk for locks, digests, signatures, SBOM/attestation, or reproducibility.

Specific Actions, AI providers, and deployment transports are replaceable adapters and do not enter the domain source of truth. Before adoption, verify permissions and behavior in a disposable repository/environment.

## 9. Project Type Adaptation

| Type | Recommended pattern | Key boundaries |
|---|---|---|
| first-party core/distribution project | Project evolves on its own; only external dependencies are reconciled item by item | Do not fabricate fork relationships; Human + independent review for core protocol and install/update behavior |
| independent first-party module | Keep an independent owner; AIOS only installs, discovers, or proxies | Do not vendor the state machine; grade protocol changes by impact |
| external core UI/service | GitHub Fork or independent repo are both acceptable; prefer adapter/managed overlay over expanding the core patch surface | update detection can be automatic; core UI upgrade, switch, and restart require separate authorization |
| private actuator | OPS facts → plan/check/diff → narrow apply → readback | inventory/secrets/production targets do not enter the public protocol; real apply is strictly limited to target scope |
| ordinary maintained fork | Choose repository topology and divergence strategy separately; expand or reduce source differences based on real friction | fetch/PR/CI can be automatic; keep a Human gate for behavioral tradeoffs, Delta retirement, and production deployment |

The AIOS registry is not a patch inventory/CMDB; actuator inventory is not the resource source of truth; a UI database/session/board is not the Matter source of truth; the same version number does not mean the same source/runtime state; a closed Issue does not mean the fix has been deployed.

## 10. Progressive Implementation

### Phase 0: On-Demand Agent Dogfooding

1. Choose one real adoption relationship.
2. The Agent automatically discovers repository/upstream/test facts, and only asks the Human about cadence, merge authority, Invariants/Deltas, and deployment boundaries.
3. Use L1 `UPSTREAM.md`, registering only 1–3 real Invariants/Deltas.
4. The user triggers the first full reconciliation; the Agent generates an isolated candidate, complete impact audit, and Decision Card.
5. After Human authorization, merge and validate to establish the automation baseline; schedule is not required at this stage.

### Phase 1: Limited Auto-Merge After Baseline and Schedule Offer

- After the baseline is established, updates that strictly satisfy R0/R1, unrelated, no unknowns, and all required checks green may use guarded auto-merge with a post-action receipt.
- The Agent proactively asks whether to enable weekly/monthly observation; if unanswered, remain on-demand.
- Scheduling only changes when reconciliation is triggered; it does not lower reconciliation, validation, or authorization gates.

### Phase 2: Automated Observation, Candidate PRs, and On-Demand AI Analysis

- Low-frequency schedules or webhooks discover immutable candidates and reuse the same reconciliation core.
- The system may automatically generate candidate branches/PRs, complete change lists, Delta/Invariant mappings, and validation evidence.
- Use AI deep review/drafting only for conflicts, overlaps, unknowns, or product tradeoffs; do not self-approve or self-merge.

Reusable tool surfaces should first be built around narrow semantics such as `status / inspect / propose / validate / apply / record`: read the project contract, generate candidates and reports, invoke existing Git/CI/PR capabilities, and do not build a new deployment system or parallel state machine. The first real project can still be handled by an Agent directly combining Git, `gh`, and project tests. Only after repeated mechanical friction appears should common CLI/CI helpers be extracted.

### Phase 3: Controlled Deployment

- Start with staging; artifact digest, health receipt, previous-known-good rollback.
- Production has separate approval; migration has a separate gate.

If the long-term cost of on-demand Agent reconciliation remains low, stopping at Phase 0/1 is success. Only add a generic validator, CLI helper, CI template, or stronger runner when real repeated friction proves the benefit; do not build an independent fork maintainer platform first.

## 11. Onboarding Checklist

- [ ] Component, Adoption, Runtime, and Local Delta owners are separated.
- [ ] repository topology and divergence strategy are declared separately; GitHub Fork is not treated as equivalent to long-term source divergence.
- [ ] Both tracking ref and accepted immutable base exist.
- [ ] Delta includes intent, behavior scope, Invariant, realization ref, and retire condition.
- [ ] cadence defaults to on-demand; automation baseline is not established before the first successful reconciliation.
- [ ] guarded auto-merge only covers R0/R1, unrelated, all-green, no-unknown changes under an established baseline.
- [ ] Git applicability and behavioral relationship are recorded separately.
- [ ] Risks R0–R4 and automation caps A0–A4 are declared.
- [ ] unknown/permission/secret/workflow/data/core-control-plane conditions fail closed.
- [ ] AI has no production secrets, mainline permissions, or production actuators.
- [ ] candidate, Decision, and validation bind exact SHA/digest.
- [ ] merge, deploy, healthy, and rollback are different states and evidence.
- [ ] Source-of-truth boundaries among project repo, registry, OPS, LLL, and GitHub do not overlap.
- [ ] Third-party Actions are pinned to full SHA and verified in a disposable environment.
- [ ] previous-known-good and rollback are verified, not only command text.

## 12. Maintenance Rules

- This document is the public protocol source of truth; skills, README, and templates are only thin entry points or instantiations.
- owner, locator, and policy/runbook refs in templates must use placeholders, public fake examples, or abstract paths; they must not contain real private instance information.
- The current active contract only expresses Deltas that still need maintenance; retirement evidence belongs in Decision/Git history, without redundant tombstones.
- After the first real project dogfood, update this document based on actual friction; do not expand the schema for imagined completeness.
- The maintenance status of tools, Actions, and providers changes over time; long-term rules only keep permission, validation, and replacement boundaries, not short-term rankings.
